questions.question: Define the evaluation function f(n) used in A* search algorithm. Explain the components g(n) and h(n), and describe how their relationship determines whether the heuristic is admissible.
keyPoints: f(n) = g(n) + h(n) where g(n) is the cost from start node to node n
h(n) is the estimated minimum cost from node n to goal (heuristic function)
Heuristic is admissible if h(n) ≤ actual cost from n to goal for all n
Admissibility guarantees A* finds the optimal (least-cost) solution

questions.question: Distinguish between admissible and consistent (monotonic) heuristics in the context of A* search. Why is consistency particularly important for A* search efficiency?
keyPoints: Admissible: h(n) never overestimates true cost from n to goal
Consistent (monotonic): h(n) ≤ cost(n, n') + h(n') for all successors n' (triangle inequality)
Consistency implies admissibility but not vice versa
Consistency ensures f(n) is non-decreasing along any path, guaranteeing no re-expansion of nodes and optimality of the explored set

questions.question: Explain the A* search algorithm in detail. Include the data structures used, the complete algorithm steps, and discuss why A* is complete and optimal when using an admissible heuristic.
keyPoints: Uses OPEN list (priority queue ordered by f(n)=g+h) and CLOSED set to track explored nodes
Algorithm: Initialize with start node, repeatedly expand node with lowest f(n), generate successors, update g(n) and add to OPEN if better path found
Optimality: With admissible heuristic, A* expands nodes in increasing f(n) order, guaranteeing first goal reached is optimal
Completeness: A* terminates if goal is reachable (finite state space, finite branching factor, positive edge costs)

questions.question: Consider a robot navigation problem on a 4×4 grid with obstacles. The robot can move in 4 directions (up, down, left, right) with unit cost. Starting at cell (0,0) and goal at cell (3,3): (a) Define Manhattan distance and Euclidean distance heuristics. (b) Analyze which heuristic is more effective and why. (c) If diagonal moves were allowed, how would your analysis change?
keyPoints: For 4-directional movement, Manhattan is admissible and dominates Euclidean (never overestimates more)
Manhattan expands fewer nodes than Euclidean, making it more efficient for grid pathfinding
With diagonal moves allowed, Chebyshev distance (max(|dx|,|dy|)) or diagonal heuristic becomes admissible and more effective
